title: Lesson 382
transcript: We just looked at the data lifecycle and we emphasized the importance of security being involved in protecting throughout each of the phases of that data lifecycle. And we actually said and emphasized that we start protecting data at the create phase. The way that we do that is by classifying it. We also said that classification, which is really the start of protecting data during its life cycle, is something that can only be done by those that are in the best position to understand the value, and that is the owners. So ownership, critically important in obviously protecting assets, including data as it starts in the create phase. That's why we need to classify data right at the create phase. In this section, we're going to expand on some of the elements that are important to keep in mind as we are trying to protect data as it goes through its life cycle. So this is referred to in the industry as managing the data lifecycle. These are some of the issues and the elements that we need to remember as data is going through those phases of its life cycle from the create phase all the way to the destruction phase. So what you're going to see here are elements that are obviously very important to keep in mind as data is going through its life cycle. We start with data collection. Even though we said the first phase in the data lifecycle is the create phase, ultimately what the organization is doing is it's collecting or deriving that data. So the important thing to remember here is that there are really two ways to collect data so that we can start protecting it at that create phase. So the two ways that really organizations can collect data and create data is first of all we can collect it through some sort of process. So we collect it through you know relationships that we have for example with our clients our customers our vendors our partners our consultants others that we have relationships with we can collect that data ultimately so that we can store it and process it to drive decisions so that we can take care of these relationships and service, for example, our clients and customers in a better way. We can collect data. The other way that we can get data is to derive it as part of some process that we have within the organization. So as part of an application, for example, that analyzes current data and comes up with dashboards and the meaning of certain data that will drive, again, better business decisions. So what we need to remember about this is that there are really two ways. to get data. One is to collect it and the other one is to derive it as part of some business process or application or system. Now, the next thing that we have to keep in mind is what's called data location. Again, these are all elements that we have to keep in mind as part of what we just looked at, which is the data lifecycle and the phases that it goes through in order for us to be able to protect it. These are all issues that we need to keep in mind so that ultimately we can protect the value of that data. So this one I think is fairly easy for us to understand. It's talking about data location. In other words, the organization has to think about the precise location of that data so that ultimately the organization can take care of really important issues like, for example, data sovereignty. and adherence to laws and regulations and privacy commitments that obviously may relate to that data that we're trying to protect. And of course, the other issue that's really important in deciding the precise location of data is the organization's ability to protect that data from a privacy perspective or a security perspective. So deciding its precise location is something that is ultimately very important to be able to take care of so that ultimately we can protect the value of that data. The terminology that the industry has been using, and you may see again, is data sovereignty. What that means is that data sovereignty is the idea that a country, or a jurisdiction has the authority and the right to govern and control the data that was generated or collected within its borders. So the idea is, and obviously a lot of countries and nations do this, they will impose rules on things like data location and where it can be stored and where it can be processed and so on in order to be able to protect really things like national security and individual privacy and data protection and so on. So data sovereignty, is this idea that a nation or a jurisdiction has the ability, should have the ability to govern and issue rules on that data that was generated or collected within its borders. And so this is something that obviously organizations have to take very seriously and be able to dictate and meet the requirements of in deciding, for example, the precise location of that data and where it is and obviously there are technological advancements and issues that may impact this like for example cloud you know having the ability to know exactly where your data is is something that is very important at any given point in time so these are all issues related to data location The next thing that we're going to talk about here is data maintenance. And so the idea behind this, as the word maintenance really implies, is that once you have data and you're storing it and processing it, there may be a need for the organization to maintain all kinds of things related to that data. Because you want to improve it. You want to improve the access and its value to the organization. So we want to maybe improve the integrity of it. and so that ultimately we can use that data in a better way to improve the business opportunity for growth and profitability. And so maintaining the data itself and the environment that it's being stored and processed and so on is what this is focused on. And the whole purpose of it is to improve the organization's agility, for example, in making business decisions and improving the reliability and the integrity of not only the data but the architecture that it is stored and processed and looked at, for example, so that decisions can be made in real time. We want to be able through data maintenance, have the ability to, for example, enhance accessibility and performance and usability of that data, right? And we want to be able to reduce inconsistencies that may exist in the current processes and architectures for that data itself. Obviously, as technology advances, we want to use the best systems, the best technologies to optimize data storage and performance of not only how to access that data, but how to interpret it and so on. And of course, really important as part of this too, we want to always have the ability to protect it based on its value. and ensure that we can protect it from a privacy perspective and from a security perspective. These are all issues that obviously cannot be done properly unless security is involved. These are all issues that we have to think about in order to be able to protect data as it goes through its life cycle that we just looked at. Now, the ones that you see at the bottom here, data retention, data remnants, data destruction. We're going to talk about in a lot more detail in some sections coming up in this course. But just to introduce him here, obviously data retention means how long do you keep data around for? And that is going to be dictated by laws and regulations related to, for example, the industry that your organization is in. All right, so health is a pretty good example of this. Health laws and regulations will dictate that you may have to keep patient information around for a very long time. And so your retention requirements, which are usually addressed through your data archiving capabilities, are going to be driven by laws and regulations. We're going to talk about that. And of course, data remnants and data destruction, these two are really connected to each other and related to each other. But what we want to be able to address from a security perspective is the issue of using good destruction methods so that ultimately we don't have what's called data remnants. If you think about these words here, data remnants, really means data remaining on some object or media that it was stored on, once we've tried to securely get rid of it. So for example, if you have a file and we simply erase it or delete it, that allows that data to really remain on that object so that potentially it can be reconstructed. And so the whole point is to prevent data remnants based on its value. So if something is really top secret, for example, or health information, we want to use a destruction method that obviously would prevent data remaining on that object based on its value. So if we relate it back to what we just talked about, which was the data lifecycle, at the end of that data lifecycle, we want to be able to use a proper destruction method that prevents that data from being able to be reconstructed by any known means so that we can ultimately prevent data remnants.
